title: Dog Ate Rat Poison? Immediate Steps & Emergency Veterinary Guide
meta_desc: If your dog ate rat poison, act now. This is a high-priority emergency. Learn why immediate vet care and product photos are critical for survival.
content_html:
TL;DR: Rat poison ingestion is a life-threatening emergency that requires immediate veterinary care, even if your dog is not currently showing symptoms.

What happens if my dog eats rat poison?

Rat poison ingestion occurs when a dog consumes rodenticides, which are chemicals designed to kill mice and rats. These substances are extremely dangerous and contain ingredients such as:

  • Anticoagulants, which cause life-threatening internal bleeding.
  • Bromethalin, which targets and affects the brain.
  • Cholecalciferol, which leads to acute kidney failure.
  • Pellets, blocks, or pastes that are often formulated to be palatable to animals.

Is rat poison ingestion a medical emergency for my dog?

Yes. The urgency level is High. Even if your dog is acting normally right now, you must seek help immediately because:

  • Many toxins do not show outward symptoms for 2 to 7 days.
  • By the time symptoms appear, significant internal damage or life-threatening bleeding may have already occurred.
  • Early intervention is the key to a successful recovery.

How does a photo of the packaging help the veterinarian?

When you contact your veterinarian or an animal poison control center, having a photo of the product packaging is incredibly helpful for the following reasons:

  • Different brands use different active ingredients, and treatment protocols vary significantly based on the toxin.
  • A clear photo of the 'Active Ingredients' list and the EPA registration number allows the medical team to identify the specific poison.
  • It enables the clinic to prepare the correct antidote or treatment plan before you even arrive.

Clinical Context (Merck Veterinary Manual)

If rodenticide exposure is suspected, obtain a thorough history, including the time of exposure, brand name, manufacturer, active ingredients and their concentration, package size, and potential amount missing; the EPA registration number is the most accurate way to confirm the active ingredient. For zinc phosphide ingestion, management relies on effective decontamination; if the animal has not already vomited, emesis can be induced with apomorphine. Decreasing gastric acid may be beneficial via oral magnesium hydroxide antacid or famotidine at 1 mg/kg SC. IV fluid support is recommended during observation. Activated charcoal may be considered, but avoid if vomiting is ongoing due to aspiration risk. Obtain a baseline biochemical profile, with repeat evaluation of liver and renal values at 24, 48, and possibly 72 hours. For anticoagulant rodenticide toxicosis, a prolonged prothrombin time (PT), activated partial thromboplastin time (APTT), or thrombin time in the presence of normal fibrinogen, fibrin degradation products, and platelet counts is strongly suggestive; stomach contents, serum, or plasma can be analyzed for anticoagulant presence.

Chapter: Toxicology, Emergency

Source: The Merck Veterinary Manual, 11th Edition (Page 3170)

faqs.question: Can I wait to see if my dog gets sick?
faqs.answer: No. Waiting for symptoms like lethargy or bleeding is dangerous. By the time symptoms appear, the toxin has likely already caused severe internal damage.

faqs.question: Should I induce vomiting at home?
faqs.answer: Do not induce vomiting unless specifically instructed to do so by a veterinarian. Depending on the toxin and how long ago it was ingested, inducing vomiting could potentially cause more harm.

faqs.question: What if I don't have the original packaging?
faqs.answer: If the packaging is gone, take a photo of the poison itself (if any remains) and try to remember the color and shape of the bait. This can still provide clues to the veterinarian.
